  • Access governance: Periodically comparing MDG role assignments with Azure AD helps identify if someone has left the organization but still holds active MDG roles. These roles can then be flagged for removal. No more inactive users remaining in governance roles.

Five use cases, one integration, one endpoint.

 

What we built and how it works

The technical setup involves SAP authenticating with Azure AD, receiving an OAuth2 token, and using that token to call the Microsoft Graph API. It requires one RFC destination for authentication, another for the API, three configuration tables, and the ABAP SDK for Azure manages the rest.

I will guide you through every step including every mistake I have gone through in the upcoming parts of this series.

What’s next?

This was the why part & Part 2 will cover the how. You'll see a complete technical walkthrough, from creating an Azure account to seeing user data appear in your SAP system

 Stay tuned.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 3)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community and #ABAPlovers

My name is Sandra Solís, an ABAP Developer with a focus on ABAP, ABAP Cloud, and modern SAP development practices. I’m passionate about building clean, efficient solutions and continuously learning new technologies within the SAP ecosystem.

From my experience in SAP projects, learning sessions, and community events, the most valuable moments are always the same: Asking questions — any kind of question — and getting honest, practical answers from people who have been there.

For this reason, I’m launching the session 3 of AMA (Ask Me Anything) series.

This space is dedicated to all things ABAP:
classic ABAP, ABAP Cloud, RAP, performance tuning, clean code, debugging, real project challenges, and even career growth in SAP development.

You are invited to ask any questions you have, openly or anonymously. No question is too simple or too complex. Let’s make this a space for sharing real experiences, solving doubts, and learning together as a community.

You can drop your question in the comments below.

or you can ask anonymously here:  Ask Me Anything Champion - Session 3 ABAP

Submit your questions during this month, and selected ones will be answered in a follow-up  video recap. Looking forward to your questions 🚀

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud
entry.content:
Getting ready for your next ABAP certification can feel like a lot.  Tight timelines, evolving concepts, and real project work happening at the same time.

That’s exactly why we designed the Partner Learning Accelerator for SAP Certified – Backend Developer – ABAP Cloud.

In this 6-hour live session, you’ll focus on what matters most.  Core ABAP concepts, the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model, and Clean Core principles in SAP S/4HANA.  Plus, practical guidance you can connect directly to your day-to-day work.

Think of it as a structured checkpoint.  You validate what you know, close the gaps, and move into the exam with more clarity.

If you’ve worked with ABAP before and want to take that next step, this session gives you a clear path forward.

Register now and move closer to certification with confidence.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010652.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #ABAP #S4HANA #TechCareers

entry.title: yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI
entry.content:
yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI

Just like every other AI tool and framework, ABAP AI tools have been evolving toward agentic AI. Over the past months, numerous features have been added, and two companion projects have been created to extend its capabilities in an optional manner, allowing you to choose what interests you and install it in your ABAP system.

ABAP AI tools now offer the option to configure AI agents by assigning system instructions, tools, and supporting documentation. Special tools have been added to enable models to load tools and documents on demand, avoiding context overload with unnecessary information. Additionally, two other groups of tools were introduced to allow agents to create plans and documents. The planning tools are particularly helpful for agent memory and for users to analyze intended actions, which can depend on approval if instructed accordingly.

Setting up AI agents in ABAP AI tools is relatively straightforward but requires maintaining some tables. Testing agents becomes more complex as conversations grow and agents begin calling tools, making it difficult to analyze their behavior. To simplify configuration and testing, a SAPUI5 cockpit was developed: the ABAP AI Tools Cockpit (detailed in the Companion Projects section below).

The next step in advancing toward agentic AI was developing tools for agents to perform actions in the system via function calling. As an ABAP developer working with a SAP AS ABAP Developer Edition system, the natural choice was to create tools for managing technical objects such as DDIC objects, message classes, translations, and transport requests. These tools were placed in a separate repository, making them optional. The following sections provide more information about these companion projects.

Companion Projects

ABAP AI tools Cockpit

The ABAP AI Tools Cockpit is a SAPUI5 application that serves as the user interface for configuring and testing AI agents using ABAP AI tools. In the Cockpit, you can set up AI agents by assigning system instructions and the tools they should have access to. Afterward, you can test the agent using the integrated chat to interact with it. In the chat view (separate from the integrated chat), you can inspect and monitor all messages and tools called by the agent. All interactions with the AI agent are visualized in a sequence diagram, making it easy to analyze the conversation flow and actions executed by the agent using the provided tools.

 

 

More about ABAP AI tools Cockpit

How to Create and Test AI Agents in the ABAP AI tools Cockpit

ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

This project contains numerous tools (ABAP class methods) that AI agents can use to interact with the ABAP system. These tools do not cover all features of the objects they handle. The goal is to provide some basic yet useful functionalities, enabling the creation of AI agents that deliver value. This allows experimentation with AI agent development and lets AI agents handle some of the “boring stuff” we need to do from time to time, like creating DDIC objects or managing translations.

More about function calling in ABAP AI tools

More about ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

Available Tools

Here are some of the available tools.

ABAP Dictionary (DDIC) Management
DDIC Objects

  • Domains (ycl_aai_fc_domain_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete domains
    • Manage fixed values
    • Manage translations for domain fixed values

  • Data Elements (ycl_aai_fc_data_element_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete data elements
    • Manage translations for data element labels

  • Structures (ycl_aai_fc_structure_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete structures

  • Tables (ycl_aai_fc_table_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete transparent tables
    • Manage technical settings (data class, size category)

  • Table Types (ycl_aai_fc_table_type_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete table types
Other objects

  • Message Classes (ycl_aai_fc_message_class_tools)

    • Create and manage message classes
    • Add, update, and delete messages
    • Handle message translations across languages

  • Text Pools (ycl_aai_fc_text_pool_tools)

    • Manage text symbols for programs/reports
    • Manage translations for text symbols and selection texts
Transport Management
  • Transport Requests (ycl_aai_fc_transport_tools)
    • Create workbench and customizing requests
    • Search and read transport request details

Explore, Experiment, and Contribute

These tools are free to use, open source, and available to you on GitHub.

Explore the repositories on GitHub:

  • yaai - ABAP AI tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Cockpit: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cockpit
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Function Calling Tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_fc

entry.title: Assistance class in WebDynpro ABAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

An Assistance Class is a special ABAP class that is allocated to a Web Dynpro component and offers reusable business logic, utility methods and data access capabilities not in the UI controller.
It assists in separation of the UI logic and business logic, as per the MVC principle.

Body:

  Generally in webdynpro ABAP we use to write all the coding part within the component itself but there is a provision with which we can write the necessary business logic outside the component that is in a class and we can use that class as the assistance class in the current component, Below are the steps involved in using this assistance class.

In the example We are creating a assistance class to get the business partner details.

Step1: Go to transaction se24 and create the class ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL as shown below, this class will contain the logic to retrieve the partner details and it will be used as the assistance class in the webdynpro component

 

 

 

Step2: Create the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL with the importing parameter as partner number and exporting parameter as partner details as shown below

 

 

Step3: Add the following code inside the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL to retrieve the partner details

Method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL.
SELECT * FROM BUT000
INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE lt_but000
WHERE PARTNER = im_partner. 
Endmethod.

 

 

Step4: The class is ready, now create the WD component ZSAMP_ASSI and provide the class name as ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL in the Assistance class field as indicated below

 



Step5:Also include the ALV component since the partner details will be displayed as ALV output, Create the view 'FIRST' with the input field element as partner number and after entering the partner number to search for the partner details include the button (search) element create the view elements as shown in the below screen

 



Step6: Make sure that the attribute WD_ASSIST is created in the FIRST view as shown below since this is the reference attribute of the assistance class which will be used to access the business logic of the class

 



Step7: After entering the partner number in the partner field the search button will be pressed so provide the following code in the event handler method of the search button to retrieve the partner details using the assistance class

method ONACTIONSEARCH .
data : lt_but000 TYPE TABLE OF but000.
data:
Elem_Context type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
Stru_Context type If_First=>Element_Context ,
Item_PARTNER like Stru_Context-PARTNER.
* get element via lead selection
Elem_Context = wd_Context->get_Element( ).
* get single attribute
Elem_Context->get_Attribute(
exporting
Name = `PARTNER`
importing
Value = Item_Partner ).
CALL METHOD WD_ASSIST->GET_PARTNER_DETAIL
EXPORTING
IM_PARTNER = Item_PARTNER
IMPORTING
LT_BUT000 = lt_but000
.
data: Node_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Node,
      Elem_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
      Stru_Partnerdetail type If_First=>Element_Partnerdetail .
* navigate from <CONTEXT> to <PARTNERDETAIL> via lead selection
      Node_Partnerdetail = wd_Context->get_Child_Node( Name =        IF_FIRST=>wdctx_Partnerdetail ).
      Node_Partnerdetail->bind_table( lt_but000 ).
endmethod.

 

 

Step8: I made here to display the name details of the partner alone in the ALV output so restrict the fields in the context node of the FIRST view as shown below

 

 

Output:
Step9: The webynpro component using the Assistance class is created now and the output for the same will be displayed as follows


Conclusion: An Assistance Class in Web Dynpro ABAP helps separate business logic from UI logic, improving code modularity and maintainability. It enables reuse of functionality across components while aligning with the MVC design principle. Above are simple step by step process  how a Assistance class can be created and how to refer assistance class and methods in webdynpro abap code

entry.title: Dynamic Field Behavior Using Side Effects
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP RAP applications, handling dynamic field behavior is a common requirement, especially when UI properties like readonly or mandatory need to change based on user input. Developers often rely on side effects to react to such changes. However, a subtle yet important gap exists,while side effects refresh field values, they do not automatically trigger feature control that governs field behavior. This can lead to confusion when expected UI changes do not occur. In this blog, we will explore this common issue and understand the correct approach to ensure that field behavior is updated dynamically using side effects and permissions.

For example, if the Status can have some data, the Description field should become mandatory and if status doesnot have any data we have to make Description field become read only.

field ( features : instance ) Description;

side effects {
field Status affects field Description;
}

However, this approach only refreshes the field value and does not impact its behavior. As a result, the feature control logic,such as setting a field to readonly, mandatory, or hidden is not triggered. This means that even though the dependent field is refreshed on the UI, its properties remain unchanged, leading to inconsistencies between the expected and actual behavior. Developers may assume that side effects automatically handle both value and behavior updates, but in reality, they only ensure data refresh. To properly influence UI behavior, an additional step is required to explicitly trigger the feature control mechanism.

Solution Overview

  • To ensure that the field behavior is updated correctly, the side effects must explicitly refresh the field permissions instead of just the field value. By doing so, the framework is instructed to re-evaluate the feature control logic, which in turn triggers the feature instance responsible for handling properties like readonly, mandatory, or visibility. This approach ensures that any changes in the dependent field are accurately reflected not only in the data but also in the UI behavior, resulting in a consistent and expected user experience.
  • Whenever Status changes, RAP will re-trigger the feature control logic for the Description field. This ensures that any conditions defined in the feature implementation (for example, making Description readonly when Status = Rejected) are applied immediately on the UI.
side effects {
field Status affects permissions ( field Description );
}

By using permissions, the feature instance is triggered, and the field behavior (such as readonly or mandatory) is updated dynamically as expected.

Interface Behavior Definition

managed implementation in class ZBP_R_TRAV_DBTT unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;
extensible;
define behavior for ZR_TRAV_DBTT alias ZrTravDbtt
persistent table ZTRAV_DBTT
extensible
draft table ZTRAV_DBTT_D
etag master LocalLastChangedAt
lock master total etag LastChangedAt
authorization master( global )
{
  field ( mandatory : create )
   TravelID;

  field ( readonly )
   LocalCreatedBy,
   LocalCreatedAt,
   LocalLastChangedBy,
   LocalLastChangedAt,
   LastChangedAt;

  field ( readonly : update )
   TravelID;

   field(features : instance) Description;
   side effects { field Status affects permissions ( field Description);}


  create;
  update;
  delete;

  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  mapping for ZTRAV_DBTT corresponding extensible
  {
    TravelID = TRAVEL_ID;
    Description = DESCRIPTION;
    Status = STATUS;
    LocalCreatedBy = LOCAL_CREATED_BY;
    LocalCreatedAt = LOCAL_CREATED_AT;
    LocalLastChangedBy = LOCAL_LAST_CHANGED_BY;
    LocalLastChangedAt = LOCAL_LAST_CHANGED_AT;
    LastChangedAt = LAST_CHANGED_AT;
  }

}

Instance Features Implementation

method get_instance_features.

  read entities of zr_trav_dbtt in local mode entity zr_trav_dbtt
    all fields with corresponding #( keys ) result data(lt_travel).

  result = value #( for entity in lt_travel
                    ( %tky = entity-%tky
                      %field-description = cond #( when entity-status is not initial
                                                   then if_abap_behv=>fc-f-mandatory
                                                   else if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only ) ) ).


endmethod.


Explanation

  • Reads current entity data (ZR_TRAV_DBTT) using provided keys into LT_TRAVEL
  • Uses IN LOCAL MODE to consider unsaved (buffered) changes
  • Loops through each record to determine field behavior dynamically
  • Maps %TKY to link feature control to the correct instance
  • Applies conditional logic on Status field
  • If Status is filled then Description becomes Mandatory
  • If Status is empty then Description becomes Read-only
  • Populates RESULT to control UI behavior at runtime
  • Works with side effects (affects permissions) to re-trigger feature logic on field change

Preview:

If the status field contains a value, then the description field becomes mandatory.

 

 

If the status field has no value, then the description field is read-only.

 

Realtime Scenario

  • RAP behavior definition to dynamically disable the Approval Status field when the Service Entry Sheet item is already approved. This ensures that once the item reaches a “valid” approved state, the field becomes read-only, preventing any further modifications and maintaining data integrity.
  • You used side effects to automatically trigger a UI refresh whenever the approval status changes. This ensures that as soon as the item is approved, the UI immediately reflects the updated state by disabling the field without requiring a manual refresh, providing a seamless and responsive user experience.

Conclusion

When working with dynamic field behavior in RAP, it is important to understand that standard side effects only refresh field values and do not trigger feature control. To ensure that properties like readonly or mandatory are updated correctly, side effects must be defined using permissions. This approach guarantees that the feature instance is executed and the UI behavior responds accurately to changes in dependent fields.

Resources

SAP HELP DOCUMENTATION: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/side-effects 

ABAP Development  SAP BTP ABAP environment

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Logger
entry.content:
Introduction

Logging in ABAP is one of those things that sounds boring until you've done it five times and realized you've written five different solutions. How many custom loggers have you written in your ABAP career? I've lost count.

The scenario keeps repeating. One developer needs structured messages, another insists on SLG1, and the business wants a JSON dump for a dashboard. Three projects later, and you’re deep in the FMs BAL_LOG_CREATE and BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD, maintaining a massive Z-class that is somehow totally different from the one you wrote for the last project.

Τhen came ABAP Cloud. Τhose old function modules weren't released anymore. SAP swapped out the BAL_* family for the cl_bali_* classes (like cl_bali_log and cl_bali_header_setter). The new API is better. Cleaner, properly object-oriented, makes more sense once you get it. The problem is getting it — there's a learning curve, and almost no community tooling around it yet.  Actually, the only one that I have found is this. Shoutout to the author( Björn Schulz )for the inspiration.

So, I decided to build a new one from scratch. With a few rules:

  • ABAP Cloud compatible. No legacy types, no unreleased APIs. Has to run on Steampunk and Embedded Steampunk natively.

  • One entry point for everything. Free text, symsg, exceptions, BAPIRET2 tables, or an arbitrary structure I want dumped to JSON — I shouldn't have to think about which method to call.

  • A fluent API. The kind where the code reads almost like a sentence.

  • Multiton instances keyed on object / subobject / external number. Two unrelated parts of the same transaction should be able to log to the same place without passing instances around, and without paying the cost of recreating loggers every time.

  • All those small things I keep hardcoding anyway. Built in, once.

    What came out of all this is ZCL_CLOUD_LOGGER, a fully open-source logger built specifically for ABAP Cloud.

    In this post, I’ll show you exactly how it works, dive into some of the architectural choices (and bugs I hit along the way), and explain how you can easily drop it into your own systems.

    Installation

    If you've used abapGit before, you already know the drill. If not, this is the moment to install abapGit. Ιt's the standard tool for ABAP open source distribution.

    The github project of cloud logger can be found here.

    The simplest possible example

    Once installed, here's what the absolute minimum looks like:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object    = 'Z_MY_LOG_OBJ'
  subobject = 'STEP_1' ).

log->log_string_add( 'Process started.' ).

" ... do stuff ...

log->log_string_add( 'Process finished.' )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Logging anything

    The standard SAP API forces you to juggle completely different calls depending on whether you're dealing with a BAPIRET2 table, an exception class, or just a standard sy-msg. My class still gives you specific methods for those, but behind the scenes, everything is routed through the exact same core logic. This guarantees your log output stays consistent, no matter what kind of data you just tossed at it. Υou can also log arbitrary data like a serialized json.

    " A symsg-style message
log->log_message_add( symsg = VALUE #( msgty = 'E'
                                        msgid = 'ZMY_MSGS'
                                        msgno = '042'
                                        msgv1 = 'Order 100' ) ).

" An exception
TRY.
    do_something_risky( ).
  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err
                            severity  = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

" Whole BAPIRET2 table — and only the errors
log->log_bapiret2_table_add( bapiret2_t   = lt_bapi_messages
                             min_severity = 'E' ).

" Log serialized to JSON automatically
log->log_data_add( data = ls_my_payload ).

    Sticky context

    Say you're looping through 50 sales orders, and each one goes through multiple validations. If one fails, your log needs to show exactly which order caused it. Without sticky context, you're forced to manually concatenate the order ID into every single string before logging it. With sticky context, you just set it once per iteration:

    log->set_context( |Order { order_id }| ).

log->log_string_add( 'validation started' ).
log->log_string_add( 'price check OK' ).
log->log_string_add( 'stock check failed' ).

log->clear_context( ).

    Built-in timing

    Every project eventually gets a requirement like "how long did this specific step take?". Since I always end up writing the same timer logic anyway, I just so I built in it directly into the logger:

    log->start_timer( ).

call_external_service( ).

log->stop_timer( 'External service call' ).

    The internal error trail

    I'll admit, almost every logger I've ever written has the same dirty secret somewhere in the code: CATCH cx_bali_runtime INTO DATA(swallowed). " ignore.You obviously don't want a simple read method like log_contains_error to trigger a dump and blow up the calling program. So, you just swallow the exception. Works fine until something actually breaks and you have no trace of what happened.

    DATA(errors) = log->get_internal_errors( ).
" Returns a table of timestamp / method_name / error_text
" for every cx_bali_runtime that was caught silently.

    Multiton config validation

    There is a specific flaw with the multiton pattern.If two callers ask for the exact same (object, subobject, ext_number) key but pass different db_save flags, the second caller just silently gets the first caller's instance. Then they waste time debugging why their db_save = abap_false log ended up being saved to the database anyway. To prevent this,  the logger checks the config and throws if there's a mismatch.

    " First caller
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_true ).

" Second caller, later in the same transaction
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_false ).
" → raises zcx_cloud_logger_error: config_mismatch

    Full Example

    Here is a full example based on a real world project:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object               = 'Z_INVOICE'
  subobject            = 'POSTING'
  enable_emergency_log = abap_true ).

log->set_context( |Invoice { invoice_id }| )
   ->start_timer( ).

TRY.
    post_invoice( invoice_id ).
    log->log_string_add( string = 'Posted successfully'
                         msgty  = log->c_message_type-success ).

  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err severity = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

log->stop_timer( 'Invoice posting' )
   ->clear_context( )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Conclusion

    Logging is the kind of code nobody notices when it works. The whole point of this class was to stop solving the same problem on every project and have something I can drop in and forget about. Fluent API, multiton, sticky context, timing, JSON dumps for the weird requirements — all the stuff I kept rebuilding by hand.

    The project is open source, MIT-licensed and runs in ABAP Cloud. I've worked through some bugs to get it stable, and it's now in real use. If you give it a try, tell me what's missing. Feel free to open an issue,pull request or just send me a message.Find it on GitHub.

entry.title: Functional Programming in ABAP – Part II: Design Strategies and Practical Patterns
entry.content:
In the first part of this series, we explored the fundamental concepts of functional programming and how they can be interpreted in the ABAP world. In this second part, we take a step further, from theory to practice, focusing on how functional programming principles can influence design strategies and patterns in Object-Oriented ABAP. The goal is simple: write code that is not only correct, but also cleaner, more expressive, and easier to evolve. In this section, we focus on design strategies and patterns that align with a functional programming style.

What defines functional programming?

In the first part, we briefly introduced functional programming. In this section, we take a deeper look at the key characteristics that define whether a programming language can be considered a functional programming paradigm. Let’s look at a few.

The theoretical underpinnings

The strictest possible definition would be based on the theoretical foundation of the language. In short, if a language uses lambda calculus as its theoretical framework, it’s a functional language.

First-class functions

A widely accepted definition of a functional programming language supports first-class functions. In practice, this means functions can be treated like any other value: they can be passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and stored in variables.

Immutability

Immutability is one of the most commonly cited characteristics of functional programming. In simple terms, it means that data does not change after it is created. For example, instead of modifying an existing structure or internal table, we create a new structure or internal table with the desired changes. The original data remains unchanged. In functional programming, values are bound to names, and once assigned, they do not change. Rather than updating a value, we create a new one. In the new ABAP syntax, it can be achieved using the following technique

FINAL(greeting) = `Hello, World!`.
Functional Purity

Functional purity refers to the property of functions, which, to be pure, must not have side effects. That is, functions are functions in the strict mathematical sense: they map inputs to outputs, but nothing else — they don’t mutate memory, they don’t perform I/O, etc.

Referential Transparency

Referential transparency is a natural result of pure functions and immutability. In simple terms, it means that a value can always be replaced with its result without changing the program's behavior. For example, if a function always produces the same output for the same input, you can safely replace the function call with its result. This makes the code easier to read and reason about, because you don’t need to track every possible execution path to understand what a value represents.

Higher-Order Functions

Higher-order functions are functions that accept other functions as arguments. This is a natural consequence of first-class functions. In the context of SAP ABAP, I have published a post explaining higher-order functions using an object-oriented approach.

Parametric polymorphism

Parametric polymorphism allows us to use so-called type variables in the type signature. At the use site, type variables are instantiated to concrete types. The implementation must be the same regardless of the specific instantiation. This last part makes it distinct from ad-hoc polymorphism, a.k.a. function overloading, which has unique implementations for each specific instantiation, and thus isn’t universal, i.e., doesn’t allow calling the overloaded function with any type, only the types the function has overloads for.

METHOD handle_parallel IMPORTING param1 TYPE ANY
                                 param2 TYPE REF TO DATA
                       RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE REF TO DATA.
Algebraic datatypes

Algebraic datatypes usually refer to the language support for product types (i.e. tuples and records), which most languages have, and sum types (a.k.a. data-carrying enums, a.k.a. discriminated unions), which are somewhat rare. They’re called algebraic because they correspond to structures in abstract algebra, but that’s mostly irrelevant to the discussion at hand — “a rose by any other name” and all that.

ADTs are indeed a prominent feature of languages like Haskell and ML. However, ultimately, it has little bearing on whether a language can be considered functional or not – it’s easy enough to imagine an imperative language with ADTs or a functional one without them. Nice to have, though.

ADTs usually come in a package with pattern matching.

Pattern matching

Pattern matching is also called “destructuring assignment” in some contexts — usually in languages that don’t have proper sum types, and thus pattern matching essentially boils down to syntactic sugar for extracting fields out of records.

There’s also a kind of pattern matching in OOP languages like Java and C# nowadays. However, there it’s closely intertwined with classes and inheritance, rather than ADTs, so similar, but different, and confusingly called the same.

Usually, in its basic form, pattern matching (at least the one we’re interested in here) comes as a case, match, &c, expression, which allows matching on specific variants of a sum type.

Is a bit of a necessity with ADTs, as an eliminator for sum types (i.e., how else would one get the value out of, say, an Option?).

result = SWITCH #( num
                  WHEN 0 THEN 1
                  WHEN 1 THEN 1
                  ELSE num * go_fact( num - 1 ) ).
Recursion

As a reminder, recursion refers to the ability of a function to call itself (directly or indirectly).

It is more or less mandatory, as functional languages generally don’t have structured constructs like loops. This directly follows from immutability and purity: indeed, updating a loop counter is a side effect. The implication is that the only way to achieve looping is recursion.

This has an important consequence, namely, a functional language has to support tail call elimination and/or guarded recursion, so as not to blow out the stack in long loops.

Expression-oriented syntax

Basically, everything is either a declaration or an expression; there are no “statements” in the traditional imperative sense, per se.

This is mostly a feature of using lambda calculus as a guiding principle, if not outright as a formal foundation of the language. Again, more of a consequence than a prerequisite, really, but it has some useful consequences, like the lack of the need for the dreaded ternary operator.

DATA(result) = REDUCE type(
  INIT var = value
  FOR ... [THEN ...] [UNTIL ...]
  NEXT var = calculation
).

DATA(lt_target) = VALUE type( ( ... ) ( ... ) ).
" With FOR
DATA(lt_target) = VALUE type( FOR ... IN ... ( ... ) ).

Design Strategy & Design Pattern

A practical way to apply functional programming principles in SAP ABAP is to clearly separate pure logic from impure operations.

Design Strategy: Separating Pure and Impure Functions

At the core of this approach is a simple idea: not all functions are equal. Some functions perform calculations and do not have any side effects, like mathematical functions, while others interact with external systems, databases, or I/O. Mixing these responsibilities often leads to code that is difficult to understand, test, and maintain.

In this design strategy, we divide functions into two categories:

  • Pure functions
    These behave like mathematical functions. Given the same input, they always produce the same output and do not cause any side effects. They do not modify global state, perform database operations, or call external services. Their responsibility is purely to transform data.
  • Impure functions 
    These functions interact with the outside world (outside the application layer at least). This includes database access, API calls, file operations, or any form of persistence. Because they depend on the external state, their behavior may vary, and they may produce side effects.

By separating these two concerns, we gain several advantages. Pure functions become easy to test, reason about, and reuse. Impure functions, on the other hand, are isolated and controlled, making the overall system more predictable.

Design Pattern: Repository + Data Service

To implement this strategy in ABAP, we can adopt a design pattern inspired by the Repository pattern, combined with a clear separation of responsibilities.

  • The Repository class represents the impure function.
    It is responsible for all interactions with external systems, such as reading from or writing to the database, calling APIs, or I/O. 
  • The Data Service class represents the pure function.
    It contains the business logic and data transformations. It receives data as input, processes it, and returns the result—without performing any side effects.

Hands-on Example: Applying The Design Pattern

Let's walk through a simple example.

Use case: Extract Customer Master Data
  • Retrieve customer master data based on user input, supporting both full load and delta load scenarios.
  • Based on the selected execution mode, the system can:
    • Display the data in ALV
    • Download the data to a local file
    • Download the data to the application server (AL11)
Step 1: Define the interfaces

Design principle: Code to Abstraction, Not Implementation.

In practice, this means we depend on interfaces, not concrete classes. Instead of tightly coupling our logic to a specific implementation, we define contracts that can be implemented in different ways. This principle becomes especially important when separating pure and impure components:

  • The impure function (repository) can have multiple implementations
    (e.g., database, API, mock for testing)
  • The pure function (data service) should not depend on how the data is retrieved and can have multiple implementations.

This is the implementation of Inclusion Polymorphism.

Define the repository interface. This interface abstracts data access and is responsible for retrieving data from the database, either through Open SQL or BAPI calls.

INTERFACE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo
  PUBLIC .
  TYPES: gty_t_company_code_range       TYPE RANGE OF bukrs,
         gty_t_customer_number_range    TYPE RANGE OF kunnr,
         gty_t_credit_ctrl_area_range   TYPE RANGE OF kkber,
         gty_t_credit_acct_range        TYPE RANGE OF knkli,
         gty_t_acct_assignment_grp_cust TYPE RANGE OF ktokd,
         gty_t_change_date_range        TYPE RANGE OF cddatum.

  TYPES: BEGIN OF gty_s_crdt_master,
           syst_id          TYPE syst_sysid,
           company_code     TYPE bukrs,
           customer_number  TYPE kunnr,
           customer_name    TYPE text100,
           credit_ctrl_area TYPE kkber,
           credit_acct      TYPE knkli,
           credit_acct_name TYPE text100,
           risk_categ       TYPE ctlpc_cm,
           term             TYPE dzterm,
           credit_limit     TYPE klimk,
           currency         TYPE waers,
           credit_blocked   TYPE crblb_cm,
           status_cust      TYPE char10,
         END OF gty_s_crdt_master,

         gty_t_crdt_master TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF gty_s_crdt_master WITH EMPTY KEY.

  METHODS find_all IMPORTING it_company_code_range       TYPE gty_t_company_code_range
                             it_customer_number_range    TYPE gty_t_customer_number_range
                             it_acct_assignment_grp_cust TYPE gty_t_acct_assignment_grp_cust
                             it_credit_ctrl_area_range   TYPE gty_t_credit_ctrl_area_range
                             it_credit_acct_range        TYPE gty_t_credit_acct_range
                             iv_credit_blocked           TYPE crblb_cm
                   RETURNING VALUE(result)               TYPE gty_t_crdt_master.

  METHODS find_delta IMPORTING it_company_code_range       TYPE gty_t_company_code_range
                               it_customer_number_range    TYPE gty_t_customer_number_range
                               it_acct_assignment_grp_cust TYPE gty_t_acct_assignment_grp_cust
                               it_credit_ctrl_area_range   TYPE gty_t_credit_ctrl_area_range
                               it_credit_acct_range        TYPE gty_t_credit_acct_range
                               it_change_date_range        TYPE gty_t_change_date_range
                               iv_credit_blocked           TYPE crblb_cm
                     RETURNING VALUE(result)               TYPE gty_t_crdt_master.

  METHODS find_new_cust IMPORTING it_company_code_range       TYPE gty_t_company_code_range
                                  it_customer_number_range    TYPE gty_t_customer_number_range
                                  it_acct_assignment_grp_cust TYPE gty_t_acct_assignment_grp_cust
                        RETURNING VALUE(result)               TYPE gty_t_crdt_master.

ENDINTERFACE.

Next, define two data service interfaces. These interfaces are responsible for handling data transformation and mapping.

INTERFACE zif_zgxxs_parser
  PUBLIC .

  METHODS parse IMPORTING iv_separator TYPE char1
                          iv_delimiter TYPE char1 OPTIONAL
                          it_source_data TYPE ANY TABLE
                          iv_include_header TYPE abap_bool DEFAULT abap_true
                RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE stringtab.

  METHODS parse_binary IMPORTING iv_separator TYPE char1
                          iv_delimiter TYPE char1 OPTIONAL
                          it_source_data TYPE ANY TABLE
                          iv_include_header TYPE abap_bool DEFAULT abap_true
                RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE xstringtab.

ENDINTERFACE.

zif_zgxxs_parser defines an abstraction for parsing data from internal table format into a text-based file format.

INTERFACE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_data_ser
  PUBLIC .

  TYPES: BEGIN OF lty_s_objectid,
           objectid TYPE cdobjectv,
         END OF lty_s_objectid,

         lty_t_objectid TYPE HASHED TABLE OF lty_s_objectid WITH UNIQUE KEY primary_key COMPONENTS objectid.

  METHODS get_updated_customer IMPORTING it_changed_docs TYPE cdredcd_tab
                               RETURNING VALUE(result)   TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_customer_number_range.

  METHODS get_key_tabs IMPORTING it_changed_docs TYPE cdredcd_tab
                       RETURNING VALUE(result)   TYPE REF TO data.

ENDINTERFACE.

zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_data_ser This interface abstracts the pure function responsible for mapping data.

Step 2: Write the Class Implementation

We now move on to the class implementation.

CLASS zcl_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo.
  PROTECTED SECTION.

    TYPES: lty_t_udate_range TYPE RANGE OF cddatum,
           lty_t_objectid_range TYPE RANGE OF cdobjectv,
           lty_t_tabname_range TYPE RANGE OF tabname,
           lty_t_fname_range TYPE RANGE OF fieldname.

    TYPES: lty_t_crdt_master TYPE SORTED TABLE OF zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_s_crdt_master WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY PRIMARY_KEY COMPONENTS status_cust.

    CONSTANTS: BEGIN OF lc_tvarv_name,
                objclass TYPE rvari_vnam VALUE 'ZGXXS_INT_CRED_MAST_OBJCLASS',
                tabname TYPE rvari_vnam VALUE 'ZGXXS_INT_CRED_MAST_TABNAME',
                fname TYPE rvari_vnam VALUE 'ZGXXS_INT_CRED_MAST_FNAME',
                tcode TYPE rvari_vnam VALUE 'ZGXXS_INT_CRED_MAST_TCODE',
               END OF lc_tvarv_name.

    DATA go_service TYPE REF TO zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_data_ser.

    METHODS get_updated_customers IMPORTING it_udate_range    TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_change_date_range
                                            it_objectid_range TYPE lty_t_objectid_range
                                  RETURNING VALUE(result)     TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_customer_number_range.

    METHODS get_changed_documents IMPORTING it_udate_range TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_change_date_range
                                            it_objectid    TYPE cdobjectv_range_tab
                                  RETURNING VALUE(result)  TYPE cdredcd_tab.

    METHODS get_variant_variables IMPORTING iv_name TYPE rvari_vnam
                                  RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE tvarvc_t.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.



CLASS ZCL_ZGXXS_CRDT_MASTER_REPO IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_changed_documents.
    DATA lt_cdred TYPE cdredcd_tab.
    DATA ls_result LIKE LINE OF result.
    DATA lt_objectid TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_data_ser=>lty_t_objectid.


    DATA(lt_objclass_range) = VALUE cdobjectcl_range_tab( FOR ls_obj IN get_variant_variables( lc_tvarv_name-objclass )
                                                          ( sign = ls_obj-sign option = ls_obj-opti
                                                            low = ls_obj-low high = ls_obj-high ) ).


    DATA(lt_tabname_range) = VALUE lty_t_tabname_range( FOR ls_tabname IN get_variant_variables( lc_tvarv_name-tabname )
                                                          ( sign = ls_tabname-sign option = ls_tabname-opti
                                                            low = ls_tabname-low high = ls_tabname-high ) ).


    DATA(lt_fname_range) = VALUE lty_t_fname_range( FOR ls_fname IN get_variant_variables( lc_tvarv_name-fname )
                                                    ( sign = ls_fname-sign option = ls_fname-opti
                                                      low = ls_fname-low high = ls_fname-high ) ).

    DATA(lt_tcode_range) = VALUE cdtcode_range_tab( FOR ls_tcode IN get_variant_variables( lc_tvarv_name-tcode )
                                                    ( sign = ls_tcode-sign option = ls_tcode-opti
                                                      low = ls_tcode-low high = ls_tcode-high ) ).

    ASSIGN it_udate_range[ 1 ] TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_l_udate>).
    IF <fs_l_udate> IS NOT ASSIGNED.
      RETURN.

    ENDIF.

    DATA(lv_date_until) = COND cddatum( WHEN <fs_l_udate>-high IS INITIAL
                                        THEN <fs_l_udate>-low
                                        ELSE <fs_l_udate>-high ).

    CALL FUNCTION 'CHANGEDOCUMENT_READ_ALL'
      EXPORTING
        i_date_of_change           = <fs_l_udate>-low    " From-change date for search
*       i_time_of_change           = '000000'    " From-change time for search
        i_date_until               = lv_date_until    " Change date up to which you want to search
*       i_time_until               = '235959'    " Latest change time in search
        it_objectclass             = lt_objclass_range    " Range Table Type for CDOBJECTCL for Function Module Interface
        it_objectid                = it_objectid
        it_tcode                   = lt_tcode_range    " Range table type for TCODE for function module interface
        it_tablename               = lt_tabname_range   " Range Table Type for CDTABNAME f. Function Module Interface
      IMPORTING
        et_cdred                   = lt_cdred    " Formatted change documents with header data
      EXCEPTIONS
        missing_input_objectclass  = 1
        missing_input_header       = 2
        no_position_found          = 3
        wrong_access_to_archive    = 4
        time_zone_conversion_error = 5
        read_too_many_entries      = 6
        OTHERS                     = 7.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
*     MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
*                WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    result = VALUE #( FOR line IN lt_cdred
                      WHERE ( fname IN lt_fname_range )
                     ( line ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_updated_customers.
  ...
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_variant_variables.

    SELECT *
      FROM tvarvc
      WHERE name eq _name
    INTO TABLE @result.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo~find_all.
    DATA result_tmp TYPE lty_t_crdt_master.

    " include new customer
    IF it_credit_ctrl_area_range IS INITIAL
      AND it_credit_acct_range IS INITIAL.

      SELECT -sysid AS syst_id
             , b~bukrs AS company_code
             , k~kunnr AS customer_number
             , a~name1 &&  && a~name2 AS customer_name
             , k~kkber AS credit_ctrl_area
             , k~knkli AS credit_acct
             , CASE k~knkli
                WHEN ' ' THEN 
                ELSE n~name1 &&  && n~name2
               END AS credit_acct_name
             , k~ctlpc AS risk_categ
             , b~zterm AS term
             , k~klimk AS credit_limit
            " Begin of changes RH0001
             , t~waers AS currency
             " End of changes RH0001
             , k~crblb AS credit_blocked
             , CASE WHEN a~sperr EQ  AND a~loevm EQ 
                    THEN 'ACTIVE'
                    ELSE 'INACTIVE'
               END AS status_cust
      FROM knb1 AS b
        INNER JOIN t001cm AS c ON b~bukrs = c~bukrs
        INNER JOIN kna1 AS a ON b~kunnr = a~kunnr
        INNER JOIN knkk AS k ON a~kunnr = k~kunnr
                            AND c~kkber = k~kkber
        LEFT OUTER JOIN t014 AS t ON k~kkber = t~kkber "#EC CI_BUFFJOIN
        LEFT OUTER JOIN kna1 AS n ON n~kunnr = k~knkli
      WHERE b~bukrs IN _company_code_range
        AND b~kunnr IN _customer_number_range
        AND a~ktokd IN _acct_assignment_grp_cust
        AND k~kkber IN _credit_ctrl_area_range
        AND k~knkli IN _credit_acct_range
      INTO TABLE @result_tmp.

      LOOP AT zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo~find_new_cust(
            it_company_code_range       = it_company_code_range
            it_customer_number_range    = it_customer_number_range
            it_acct_assignment_grp_cust = it_acct_assignment_grp_cust
        ) INTO DATA(ls_new_cust).

        INSERT ls_new_cust INTO TABLE result_tmp.

      ENDLOOP.


    ELSE.

      SELECT -sysid AS syst_id
             , b~bukrs AS company_code
             , k~kunnr AS customer_number
             , a~name1 &&  && a~name2 AS customer_name
             , k~kkber AS credit_ctrl_area
             , k~knkli AS credit_acct
             , CASE k~knkli
                WHEN ' ' THEN 
                ELSE n~name1 &&  && n~name2
               END AS credit_acct_name
             , k~ctlpc AS risk_categ
             , b~zterm AS term
             , k~klimk AS credit_limit
             , t~waers AS currency
             , k~crblb AS credit_blocked
             , CASE WHEN a~sperr EQ  AND a~loevm EQ 
                    THEN 'ACTIVE'
                    ELSE 'INACTIVE'
               END AS status_cust
      FROM knb1 AS b
        INNER JOIN t001cm AS c ON b~bukrs = c~bukrs
        INNER JOIN kna1 AS a ON b~kunnr = a~kunnr
        INNER JOIN knkk AS k ON a~kunnr = k~kunnr
                            AND c~kkber = k~kkber
        LEFT OUTER JOIN t014 AS t ON k~kkber = t~kkber "#EC CI_BUFFJOIN
        LEFT OUTER JOIN kna1 AS n ON n~kunnr = k~knkli
      WHERE b~bukrs IN _company_code_range
        AND b~kunnr IN _customer_number_range
        AND a~ktokd IN _acct_assignment_grp_cust
        AND k~kkber IN _credit_ctrl_area_range
        AND k~knkli IN _credit_acct_range
      INTO TABLE @result_tmp.

    ENDIF.

    result = SWITCH #( iv_active_cust_only
                       WHEN abap_false THEN CORRESPONDING #( result_tmp )
                       ELSE FILTER #( result_tmp USING KEY primary_key
                                      WHERE status_cust = CONV #( 'ACTIVE' ) ) ).


  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo~find_delta.

    DATA lt_result TYPE SORTED TABLE OF zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_s_crdt_master WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY primary_key
                                                                                      COMPONENTS customer_number company_code credit_ctrl_area.
    DATA lt_result_kna1 TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_crdt_master.
    DATA lt_result_knb1 TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_crdt_master.
    DATA lt_result_knkk TYPE zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_t_crdt_master.
    DATA lt_result_merge TYPE SORTED TABLE OF zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo=>gty_s_crdt_master WITH NON-UNIQUE KEY primary_key
                                                                                      COMPONENTS customer_number company_code credit_ctrl_area.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS <lfs_t_keytab> TYPE zcl_zgxxs_crdt_master_service=>gty_t_keytab.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS <lfs_t_keytab_kna1> TYPE zcl_zgxxs_crdt_mast_serv_kna1=>gty_t_keytab_kna1.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS <lfs_t_keytab_knb1> TYPE zcl_zgxxs_crdt_mast_serv_knb1=>gty_t_keytab_knb1.
    go_service = NEW zcl_zgxxs_crdt_master_service( ).
    " get changed documents
    DATA(lt_changed_docs) = get_changed_documents(
                            it_udate_range = it_change_date_range
                            it_objectid    = VALUE #( FOR line IN it_customer_number_range
                                                      ( sign = line-sign option = line-option low = line-low high = line-high ) )
                        ).
    IF lt_changed_docs IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.

    ENDIF.

    " get updated customers
    DATA(lt_updated_customers) = go_service->get_updated_customer( lt_changed_docs ).
    IF lt_updated_customers IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.

    ENDIF.

    " extract data
    lt_result = CORRESPONDING #( zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo~find_all(
       EXPORTING
         it_company_code_range       = it_company_code_range
         it_customer_number_range    = lt_updated_customers
         it_acct_assignment_grp_cust = it_acct_assignment_grp_cust
         it_credit_ctrl_area_range   = it_credit_ctrl_area_range
         it_credit_acct_range        = it_credit_acct_range
         iv_active_cust_only         = iv_active_cust_only
     ) ).
    IF lt_result IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.

    ENDIF.

    DATA(lo_keytab) = go_service->get_key_tabs( lt_changed_docs ).
    ASSIGN lo_keytab->* TO <lfs_t_keytab>.
    IF <lfs_t_keytab> IS ASSIGNED
      AND <lfs_t_keytab> IS NOT INITIAL.

      lt_result_knkk = FILTER #( lt_result IN <lfs_t_keytab> USING KEY primary_key
                          WHERE customer_number = kunnr
                            AND credit_ctrl_area = kkber ).

    ENDIF.

    go_service = NEW zcl_zgxxs_crdt_mast_serv_kna1( ).
    DATA(lo_keytab_kna1) = go_service->get_key_tabs( lt_changed_docs ).
    ASSIGN lo_keytab_kna1->* TO <lfs_t_keytab_kna1>.
    IF <lfs_t_keytab_kna1> IS ASSIGNED
      AND <lfs_t_keytab_kna1> IS NOT INITIAL.

      lt_result_kna1 = FILTER #( lt_result IN <lfs_t_keytab_kna1> USING KEY primary_key
                                 WHERE customer_number = kunnr ).

    ENDIF.

    go_service = NEW zcl_zgxxs_crdt_mast_serv_knb1( ).
    DATA(lo_keytab_knb1) = go_service->get_key_tabs( lt_changed_docs ).
    ASSIGN lo_keytab_knb1->* TO <lfs_t_keytab_knb1>.
    IF <lfs_t_keytab_knb1> IS ASSIGNED
      AND <lfs_t_keytab_knb1> IS NOT INITIAL.

      lt_result_knb1 = FILTER #( lt_result IN <lfs_t_keytab_knb1> USING KEY primary_key
                                 WHERE customer_number = kunnr
                                   AND company_code = bukrs ).

    ENDIF.

    lt_result_merge = CORRESPONDING #( lt_result_kna1 ).

    LOOP AT lt_result_knb1 INTO DATA(ls_result_knb1).
      IF NOT line_exists( lt_result_merge[ KEY primary_key
                                           COMPONENTS customer_number = ls_result_knb1-customer_number
                                                      company_code = ls_result_knb1-company_code
                                                      credit_ctrl_area = ls_result_knb1-credit_ctrl_area ] ).

        INSERT ls_result_knb1 INTO TABLE lt_result_merge.

      ENDIF.

    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT lt_result_knkk INTO DATA(ls_result_knkk).
      IF NOT line_exists( lt_result_merge[ KEY primary_key
                                           COMPONENTS customer_number = ls_result_knkk-customer_number
                                                      company_code = ls_result_knkk-company_code
                                                      credit_ctrl_area = ls_result_knb1-credit_ctrl_area ] ).

        INSERT ls_result_knkk INTO TABLE lt_result_merge.

      ENDIF.

    ENDLOOP.

    result = CORRESPONDING #( lt_result_merge ).


  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo~find_new_cust.

    SELECT -sysid AS syst_id
           , b~bukrs AS company_code
           , b~kunnr AS customer_number
           , a~name1 &&  && a~name2 AS customer_name
           , b~zterm AS term
           , CASE WHEN a~sperr EQ  AND a~loevm EQ 
                  THEN 'ACTIVE'
                  ELSE 'INACTIVE'
             END AS status_cust
    FROM knb1 AS b
      INNER JOIN t001cm AS c ON b~bukrs = c~bukrs
      INNER JOIN kna1 AS a ON b~kunnr = a~kunnr
    WHERE b~bukrs IN _company_code_range
      AND b~kunnr IN _customer_number_range
      AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT kunnr
                        FROM knkk AS kn  "#EC CI_BUFFSUBQ
                        WHERE kn~kunnr eq b~kunnr
              )
      AND a~ktokd IN _acct_assignment_grp_cust
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @result.

  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

The class zcl_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo implements the interface zif_zgxxs_crdt_master_repo and is responsible for retrieving data from the database. As it performs data access operations, this class represents the impure function, where side effects are introduced.
